The differential equation provided combines logistic growth with a time-dependent seasonal forcing term:
\[ \frac{dP}{dt} = 0.3P \left(1 - \frac{P}{1000}\right) + 0.1 \sin\left(\frac{\pi t}{6}\right)P \]
This can be rewritten as:
\[ \frac{dP}{dt} = P \left[ 0.3 \left(1 - \frac{P}{1000}\right) + 0.1 \sin\left(\frac{\pi t}{6}\right) \right] \]
\[ \frac{dP}{dt} = P \left[ 0.3 + 0.1 \sin\left(\frac{\pi t}{6}\right) - \frac{0.3}{1000}P \right] \]
This is a first-order nonlinear ordinary differential equation of the Bernoulli type with a time-varying coefficient. Specifically, it takes the form \( \frac{dP}{dt} = (a(t) - b(t)P)P \), where \( a(t) = 0.3 + 0.1 \sin(\frac{\pi t}{6}) \) and \( b(t) = \frac{0.3}{1000} \).

To find the general solution, we use the substitution \( u = \frac{1}{P} \), which implies \( \frac{du}{dt} = -\frac{1}{P^2} \frac{dP}{dt} \).
Substituting the original equation:
\[ \frac{dP}{dt} = a(t)P - b(t)P^2 \]
\[ -P^2 \frac{du}{dt} = a(t)P - b(t)P^2 \]
Dividing by \( -P^2 \):
\[ \frac{du}{dt} = -a(t)u + b(t) \]
\[ \frac{du}{dt} + a(t)u = b(t) \]
This is a linear first-order differential equation. The integrating factor is \( I(t) = \exp\left(\int a(t) dt\right) \).
